data.title: Karyopharm: Putting The Phase 3 Trial In Context
data.description:
Topline Summary and Update

I've followed Karyopharm Therapeutics (KPTI) for quite a while now with optimism that has not matched reality, placing "Buy" sentiments on the company in 2024 and then later that same year, only to see the valuation of the stock decline by more than 50% as the market has awaited the results of a key, arguably existential phase 3 trial. We now have a first look at the results of that trial, and the market has not been pleased, sending the share price from a high of $8.90 to the near-$5 price point that it sits in at the time of writing. Today, I want to take another look at the company, with the intention of outlining why I think there is reason for optimism here.

Pipeline Updates

Selinexor

For years now, KPTI has been working ardently toward finding new niches for their XPO1 inhibitor selinexor outside of the multiple myeloma space. A readout for endometrial cancer remains a bit on the horizon, with the latest guidance from management suggesting that results should be coming in mid-2026. This relates to the phase 3 XPORT-EC-042 trial evaluating selinexor as a maintenance therapy for TP53 wildtype endometrial cancer; recall that this trial builds on a signal from the SIENDO study, which met its PFS endpoint but was not considered clinically relevant, but a signal of major benefit was identified in the TP53-wildtype subgroup of patients.

So we will wait for the results of XPORT-EC-042 with bated breath. The news we have today relates to a topline readout of the phase 3 SENTRY trial, which compared the addition of selinexor to ruxolitinib versus ruxolitinib alone for patients with treatment-naive myelofibrosis.

The trial met only one of its two primary endpoints. Adding selinexor improved the rate of patients achieving a 35% reduction in spleen size (rates of 50% and 28% for combination and ruxolitinib alone, respectively), but the co-primary endpoint of total symptom score was not improved (9.89 vs 10.86 point improvement).

This actually echoes the findings from MANIFEST-2, a phase 3 study evaluating the addition of the BET inhibitor pelabresib to ruxolitinib in a similar group of patients. Pelabresib plus ruxolitnib had a spleen reduction rate of 65.9% vs 35.2% for ruxolitinib alone, but total symptom score was not different. Notably, pelabresib has not been pushed forward to getting FDA approval at this time, and Novartis is spinning up another phase 3 trial to try pelabresib in patients with higher-risk disease.

For the SENTRY trial, KPTI has seen an interesting signal of improved overall survival with the use of selinexor, with 4.7% vs 10.2% of patients having died in the experimental and control arms, respectively (stratified hazard ratio, 0.43; one-sided nominal P value 0.0222). Median overall survival was 11.56 and 12.58 months, respectively, reflecting how immature these data are.

Reduction of the JAK2 variant allele fraction (a marker of how persistent the disease is) was also observed more frequently with selinexor (32% vs 24%).

Tolerabiliy, as has long been the case with selinexor, was a snag for the study. Nausea rates were increased from 17.2% to 57.3%, and the rate of discontinuing treatment due to AEs increased from 8.6% to 14.5%. Neutropenia and thrombocytopenia rates were also elevated with selinexor therapy.

As part of the presentation, KPTI brought in MPN expert John Mascarenhas to discuss the results, who noted that the overall survival signal was encouraging, and in line with known predictors of overall survival (ie, higher spleen volume response rate, reduction in JAK2 allele burden). He ended with the following:

I believe the combination should be available to all patients, although this may not be the preferred treatment selection for every patient. I look forward to presenting the greater data set from the pivotal trial at upcoming meetings.

That summarizes what is a decided mixed bag of results that KPTI is moving forward with bringing to the FDA for potential approval consideration, per their presentation of the results. A final timeline for their plan toward a supplemental NDA is anticipated within the next 6 months.

Financial Overview

As of the end of 2025, KPTI held $60.5 million in cash and equivalents, along with $3.2 million in investments. They had a senior secured term loan of $21.0 million, as well as various convertible senior notes totaling $111 million. Total revenue for the year was $146.1 million, more or less the same as it was in 2024 and 2023. Operating expenses were $236.8 million, and after interest and extinguishment of debt, the annual net loss was $196 million.

The net cash used for operations was $75.4 million. At this burn rate, KPTI has under one year of funds to continue. This was before the proceeds of an anticipated private placement with RA capital that will bring in $30 million in exchange for shares and warrants.

Strengths and Risks

Mixed - A path to approval, but not one that's certain

I have to be honest: this phase 3 trial is a tough one. Missing one of the primary endpoints is not a good sign for ultimate approval. And that has played out pretty poorly for pelabresib, now finding itself tied to more years of study before it could potentially be added to the mix.

Selinexor appears to be in a similar boat, winning on spleen volume but missing on symptoms. And add the toxicity to boot, and the deck looks stacked against a possible approval.

But this phase 3 trial isn't "tough" because I'm disappointed in the results. It's tough because they have a signal of overall survival improvement that looks encouraging. Even after 96 weeks follow-up for MANIFEST-2, overall survival looked almost completely the same down the line. SENTRY appears to have something to it, and I could potentially see a conditional approval coming on the basis of the positive primary endpoint and this overall survival signal.

However, that's very much not clear to happen at this point, and that does not bode well for the near-term prospects of the investment thesis.

Risk - Dire cash situation even after their private placement

Even with over $100 million in net product revenues, KPTI is facing a cash crisis, and their own guidance during the last corporate update put their runway only "into Q2 2026." Certainly, the $30 million private placement is a shot in the arm, but KPTI has not definitively solved their cash issue with this move, and even under the best circumstances with the FDA, they are looking at a potential approval not coming sooner than late in 2026, and that would be quite an optimistic timeline. How exactly they will continue to fund operations beyond 2026 has not yet been determined, and that makes for a highly risky situation for the investment thesis.

Bottom-Line Summary

KPTI is entering yet another period of uncertainty following the readout of a key phase 3 trial. Will SENTRY yield a new drug approval for selinexor? It's not possible to tell at this point. It's definitely in play, but it's not great to feel this amount of uncertainty about the prospects for this agent.

Couple that with the rather short cash runway that KPTI holds, and it's very easy to see why the market has reacted with substantial pessimism about the value of this stock. And that, honestly, presents the bulk of the opportunity here. KPTI is beaten down, but they now have multiple potential catalysts on deck for 2026 that could present a reversal of fortunes. We will see an initial readout of the endometrial cancer study. We'll get more information about the myelofibrosis study. Positive interactions with the FDA around myelofibrosis, or a positive readout for endometrial cancer (which would, recall, just be a confirmation of the signal from the subgroup analysis of SIENDO) would very likely bring a substantial positive re-evaluation from the market.

And for that reason, I continue to see the near-term upside potential as being worthy of a tentative "Buy" sentiment. That being said, you should not read that as an "unequivocal" recommendation. KPTI is entering into territory that poses existential risks. They have less than a year of cash on hand, and that is not enough time to get to approval. It is likely that they will need to raise funds to continue operations, and there is no guarantee they'll be able to do that.

Therefore, although I am reiterating my "Buy" sentiment at these levels, I would absolutely caution against going too deep into it. And it should be pointed out that the average volume of the stock is relatively low at around 1.2 million shares, which could impede one's ability to enter or exit a position.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Why Credo's Selloff Is A Massive Gift
data.description:
As the AI infrastructure buildout has progressed, the market has focused primarily on the likes of Nvidia (NVDA), AMD (AMD), and Micron (MU) - top semiconductor companies that produce the chips that drive cutting-edge AI models forward. Nvidia's top chips command tens of thousands apiece on the open market, and Micron has recently benefited from a massive shortage in the memory space.

What often gets overlooked are the cables that tie AI data centers together. Fast chips are good, but they're only as good as their ability to talk to other pieces of the cluster.

Enter Credo Technologies (CRDO). As a leading seller of AECs, or "Active Electrical Cables," the firm has been a direct beneficiary of the recent data center buildout. The company's products allow for efficient data center optimization - especially over short distances - and revenue is up 6x over the last two years.

However, following the company's most recent Q3 earnings report (when management announced that sales were up 201% year-over-year), the stock sold off nearly 20%.

Why?

A perceived deceleration in Credo's growth story. On the call, management expressed that forward revenue growth for fiscal year 2027 would "only" be in the ~50% range.

This recent loss compounds with poor year-to-date performance of -30%. All told, I view this dip as a massive gift from the market.

With top and bottom line results set to expand rapidly over the next few years - especially as new products expand the total addressable market - the business is set up to produce significant appreciation for investors.

Trading at a reasonable price versus itself and key peers, I'm anticipating 30%+ annualized total returns through the end of the decade.

Today, I'll break down the company's prospects, touch on the valuation, and make the case that this recent dip in CRDO is a huge opportunity that you should take advantage of.

Financials

In case you haven't heard of Credo before, as I just referenced, the business primarily makes and sells active electrical cables, or AECs, for use in AI data centers. These AECs connect servers and racks, allowing for 1,000x greater link reliability and 50% lower power requirements versus traditional cabling.

In addition to AECs, the company also sells a number of semiconductor-focused integrated circuits, including retimers, DSPs, and chiplets. Tying it all together, CRDO also has a small IP portfolio that generates marginal revenue.

Financially, Credo is growing like a weed. In Q3 of fiscal 2026 (covering November to January), CRDO reported revenue of $407 million, year-over-year growth of 201%, and quarter-over-quarter growth of roughly 52%:

This revenue acceleration ties directly into the pure-play exposure that CRDO has to the data center capex cycle. As big cloud companies like Microsoft (MSFT), Google (GOOG), and Amazon (AMZN) outfit new data centers across the world, they need efficient cables to connect servers together, and CRDO is becoming a favored supplier for this niche.

With structurally strong gross margins coming in at nearly 70%, CRDO is a rare "one-two" punch of a company sporting strong margins and strong growth:

This, in turn, gives management incredible optionality when it comes to deploying fresh capital, and with a debt-free balance sheet, new money coming in is poised to drop directly to the bottom line.

With strong product-market fit and a dialed-in business model, it's no surprise that net income has grown from -$10 million in Q1 of fiscal 2025 to +$157 million in Q3 of fiscal 2026:

Even more importantly, management has been focused on developing new product pillars, including ZF Optics (Zero-Flat), Active LED Cables (ALCs), and OmniConnect gearboxes. Combined, each of these products targets a total addressable market exceeding $10 billion. And, with ongoing momentum and credibility from key hyperscaler customers, I expect these new product launches in fiscal 2027 and 2028 to be received well by the market.

This all adds up to a robust long-term growth outlook.

Currently, I'm modeling 205% growth in fiscal 2026, 52% growth in fiscal 2027, and 27% growth in fiscal 2028. From there, I'm expecting a re-acceleration in sales as Credo's new products begin to cash flow:

Analysts - for their part - map out a relatively similar path.

Valuation

This all maps out to an attractive valuation situation for the stock.

As I alluded to at the start of the article, shares have sold off considerably over the last few months as CRDO sold shares to raise cash, and the deceleration in earnings may have spooked away some institutional money.

However, I view this dip as a massive gift for long-term-oriented folks.

In terms of public comps, I see CRDO's closest peers as Astera Labs (ALAB), Marvell (MRVL), Broadcom (AVGO), and Arista Networks (ANET). At present, these companies trade at an average trailing twelve-month price-to-sales ratio of 18.7x:

Even if you take out Astera Labs, which is by far the most expensive company in the group, this is still an expensive-looking industry, trading at roughly 17.3x sales:

At present, CRDO is trading at the midpoint of the group at 17.8x sales. If we assume a strong finish to the year, then this could shrink further to 14.3x sales, assuming the stock holds at a stable price:

Long-term, if we take CRDO's growth trajectory and assume a 14x sales multiple as a reasonable steady-state expectation, then shares of CRDO could produce roughly 30% annualized returns over the next 48 months:

Zooming out more broadly, I think that a fair value range for this cohort is anywhere from 12.5x to 22x sales, based on historical growth rates, gross margins, and more. For CRDO, this implies a price target of anywhere between $160 and $283 over the next 24 months, representing growth of anywhere between 23% and 61% annualized:

Between the company's strong execution, reasonable entry multiple, and attractive exposure to this technological megatrend, I'm reiterating my "Strong Buy" rating on Credo.

Risks

Of course, there are a number of risks worth mentioning.

First, and most importantly, CRDO has heavy revenue concentration with just a handful of customers within the cloud industry. This exposes the business to changes at just a few companies, in addition to a wider slowdown in cyclical capex.

In Q3, two customers made up roughly 71% of revenue (at 39% and 32%), and a single in-house development push, pause, or architecture decision could cut CRDO out of the value chain, hitting revenue severely. In recent quarters, the company has sought to reduce this risk and recently onboarded another hyperscaler client, bringing the total to five. But broadly, this is the most pressing risk for investors.

Secondly, there are some other risks worth mentioning. On the valuation side, while CRDO is priced reasonably for the semiconductor space, it is relatively expensive versus the broader market, and in the event of a sharp downturn, I could see shares of CRDO getting battered purely as a result of the multiple.

Additionally, high stock-based compensation remains a concern, with SBC representing nearly 20% of the company's overall cost structure:

This promises to dilute shareholders, which could weigh on returns for investors.

Summary

Overall, I view CRDO as an incredibly high-growth opportunity that most investors should be taking advantage of. The recent selloff represents a deceleration after an unbelievable period of growth - not a structural problem with the wider story.

Of course, the company has a number of risks, including significant customer concentration risk, but I view shares - at only 14x blended sales - as an unbelievable deal.

Thus, my "Strong Buy" rating.

Cheers!

data.title: Gold Bearish Trend Resumes Below $4,620 As Stagflation And Oil Strength Weigh
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

Since March 19, gold (XAUUSD:CUR) has staged the expected bearish impulsive down move sequence and plummeted by 15% to print a 4-month low of $4,099 on Monday, March 23, 2026, supported by the “stagflation fear” macro factor.

Thereafter, the previous yellow metal staged a rebound of 12% to hit an intraday high of $4,603 on the backdrop of “TACO” optimism that the US White House Administration is looking to end the month-long US-Iran war, in turn allowing passage to reopen in the Strait of Hormuz, the global oil flow choke point.

Read more over here why "TACO"-induced risk-on sentiment may fail to materialize this time: “Global Markets Swing On U.S.-Iran War Headlines As Risk-On Rally Falters - A Cross Analysis On S&P 500, U.S. Dollar Index, AUD/USD, And WTI Crude”.

Right now, intermarket and technical analyses are pointing to another leg of bearish impulsive down move for gold, likely the end of the 12% corrective rebound, aka dead cat bounce, from Monday’s low.

A firmer WTI crude oil supports further weakness in gold

The macro connection between WTI crude oil and gold is stagflation risk

Higher oil prices via supply-side shock (closure of the Strait of Hormuz leads to a lesser oil supply globally, in turn also creating a second-order effect of lower aggregate demand as input costs of finalized goods and services get more expensive).

Hence, stagflation is a deadly combination of higher prices and lower economic growth prospects in later stages. A challenging environment for central bankers as they cannot easily implement expansionary monetary policies to counter and anticipate the second-order demand destruction in a stagflation environment.

Therefore, central banks are likely to adopt a “wait and see” approach, and some “inflation-fighting” central banks may turn cautiously hawkish and start to implement an interest rate hike cycle.

Gold, being a non-interest income-bearing asset, will incur higher opportunity costs as interest rates rise globally, in turn triggering a negative feedback loop into the price actions of gold.

Since February 17, 2026, the movement of WTI crude oil futures has had an indirect correlation with gold, and its 20-day rolling correlation coefficient stands at -0.5 at this time of writing (see Fig.1).

The recent pullback in the West Texas Oil CFD (a proxy of the WTI crude oil futures) due to “TACO jawbowning” has managed to find support at its rising 20-day moving average.

West Texas Oil CFD’s medium-term uptrend phase remains intact. A clearance above $93.70 key near-term resistance may see a further push up to retest the $102.25 intermediate range resistance in the first step (see Fig. 2).

Gold - End of corrective rebound, start of new bearish leg

Watch the $4,620 key short-term pivotal resistance on gold. A break below the $4,440 key near-term support (downside trigger level) may set off another bearish impulsive down move sequence to retest $4,167/4,099 before exposing the next supports at $4,007 and $3,936/3,886 (also a Fibonacci extension) (see Fig. 3).

On the other hand, a clearance and an hourly close above $4,620 invalidate the bearish scenario for an extension of the corrective rebound towards the $4,737/4,775 key medium-term pivotal resistance zone.

Key elements to support the bearish bias on gold

  • The hourly RSI momentum indicator has staged a bearish breakdown below its key ascending trendline support.
  • The recent 12% rebound seen in gold from its March 23, 2026 low has stalled close to the 50% Fibonacci retracement of the prior impulsive down move from the March 10, 2025 high to the March 23, 2026 low.

Original Post

data.title: EquipmentShare.com: A Much More Compelling Set-Up Here
data.description:
When EquipmentShare.com (EQPT) went public in January, I concluded that rental growth came at a price. Investors liked the rapid growth and philosophy of this rental company, self-described as the future of construction, yet I feared slim operating margins and quite some debt employed, leaving no realistic earnings.

Ever since, a $33 stock has fallen about 40% to its current level at $20 per share; that is less than two months following the offering. This big pullback more than warrants an update, certainly as the company just reported its 2025 results, with the company ending the year on a solid note.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent IPOs, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

About These Results

In the second half of March, EquipmentShare.com reported its 2025 results. Total revenue growth was reported at 16%, with full-year sales reported at $4.38 billion, but that understates the real growth. Equipment rental revenue rose by 34% to $2.72 billion, while equipment sales fell 8% to $1.54 billion. All this was complemented by some smaller equipment parts and supplies revenues. Key rental revenue showed strong growth throughout the year, ending the fourth quarter with 35% growth.

Operating profits of $297 million came in at nearly 7% of sales. Such operating earnings were up 36% on the year before, amidst fundamental margin gains demonstrated upon. That was mostly sufficient to pay a $285 million interest bill, leaving flattish results on the bottom line. That is not really fair, as the company is in massive expansion mode, having opened 95 new sites during 2025, to end the year with 385 operational locations. In fact, the company quantified new market startup costs at $252 million, a huge number in the grand scheme of things.

Growth does not come just at an expense in terms of startup costs, but certainly capital spending and related assets as well. Original equipment costs under management rose to $8.8 billion, but focus on the word "managed" instead of "owned." Net capital spending, net of sales, was seen at just over $600 million last year. Some equipment, about half of the assets, is financed with the "OWN" program, essentially a way to keep some of these assets off-balance sheet.

Net leverage of 3.2 times as of the end of 2025 was seen down to 2.4 times, factoring in proceeds from the IPO, with pro forma net debt seen at $2.34 billion. Note that to arrive at this leverage number, the company adds back a quarter of a billion in new market startup costs to reported EBITDA, that being a fairly aggressive practice.

More Growth

The company sees growth slowing in the number of potential locations in 2026, seen at a midpoint of 425, which suggests 40 net openings, a number more than cut in half from 2025. Total revenues are seen up to $5.05-$5.47 billion, with rental revenues seen between $3.31 and $3.59 billion, all looking quite solid. Despite the slower projected pace of rental growth at 27%, net capital spending is seen increasing to nearly $800 million.

While all this looks good, the adjusted core EBITDA metric is soft. This is seen at a midpoint of $1.87 billion in 2026, just up 12% on the year before, with this number lagging rental growth percentages by more than half, indicating some margin pressure. All this indicates that the business will see rapid growth, but realistic earnings likely will not be seen in 2026 as well. Note that this metric for EBITDA is much larger, this adding the EBITDA contribution from the OWN program asset as well, which is roughly half of total assets, these essentially being kept off-balance sheet.

By now, 251 million shares have fallen some $13 since I covered shares in January, shedding well over $3 billion in value, that being a big pullback here. Currently, the market value stands at $5.0 billion at $20 per share, and including net debt of $2.4 billion, the enterprise valuation stands at $7.4 billion.

This $7.4 billion enterprise valuation comes in at 2.7 times rental revenues and just over 2 times rental revenues based on the guidance. This starts to look a lot more compelling here, as undisputed industry leader United Rentals (URI) is valued at just over 4 times rental revenues, but posts operating income around 25% (that includes both rental revenue and sales of rental equipment).

What Now?

The reality is that I like the approach of the business, focusing heavily on providing innovation to the building site. Suffering from idled machines and outdated (paper) processes, the industry has plenty of opportunity to improve utilization rates with dynamic prices, smart scheduling, and preventive maintenance performed with real-time data, purpose-built software, and related processes.

Given the discussion above, there are some things to like. For one, is continued growth in the final quarter, but moreover, a setback in expectations following a 40% pullback in the share price. While the company is essentially breaking even, I like the continuation of growth in 2026, yet the divergence (with much lower adjusted EBITDA growth) is worrying.

This makes me take a wait-and-see approach here, yet I feel more comfortable at current lower levels. Break-even levels are achieved, while earnings are depressed by growth initiatives and the fact that relative sales multiples now look compelling (recognizing the huge margin gap, of course), as I like the organic growth reported here. On the negative side, real earnings are not yet seen, as the OWN Program complicates things a bit.

Weighing it all together, I am turning more upbeat here, mostly given the lower expectations. However, the discrepancy between core adjusted EBITDA growth and the lagging sale price is significant. I am not convinced to buy the dip just yet, looking for more clues about these margin trends as 2026 progresses.

data.title: Money Supply Grows At Fastest Pace Since 2021
data.description:
Money supply is a very important indicator. It helps show how tight or loose current monetary conditions are regardless of what the Fed is doing with interest rates. Even if the Fed is tight, if money supply is increasing, it has an inflationary effect.

One key metric shown below is the “Wenzel” 13-week annualized money supply figure. It was made popular by the late Robert Wenzel who tracked the metric weekly as an indicator for where the economy might be headed. In 2020, the Fed started reporting the data monthly instead of weekly. It should also be noted that money supply data can be heavily revised in future months.

Recent Trends

Seasonally adjusted money supply has been growing on a consistent monthly basis since November 2023 (28 straight months). The latest month (February) showed a massive increase of $198B. This is more than double the recent trend, which is quite alarming. What has caused this sudden increase in money supply? It likely has many drivers, but the fact the Fed has restarted QE could be a major reason.

The increase in February was 11.1% annualized, which is well above the 4.9% over the past year.

February average is typically around 6%, which puts the latest month still well above average.

Non-seasonally adjusted shows negative growth for the month of January and positive growth for February and March (note: this data is ahead of the seasonally-adjusted data above).

The weekly data shows what happened: the recent data has been moving with more consistency rather than exhibiting a more choppy behavior as it did for most of the past.

The “Wenzel” 13-week Money Supply

The late Robert Wenzel of Economic Policy Journal used a modified calculation to track money supply. He used a trailing 13-week average growth rate annualized as defined in his book The Fed Flunks. He specifically used the weekly data that was not seasonally adjusted. His analogy was that in order to know what to wear outside, he wants to know the current weather, not temperatures that have been averaged throughout the year.

The objective of the 13-week average is to smooth some of the choppy data without bringing in too much history that could blind someone from seeing what’s in front of them. The 13-week average growth rate can be seen in the table below.

Growth has been relatively flat for several weeks now, around the 6% range.

The plot below shows how the latest year compares with previous years. Typically, this is the time of year when money supply growth starts to dip. However, it seems that this year is remaining much more flat.

Inflation and Money Supply

The chart below shows the history of inflation, money supply, and Fed funds. As shown, in 1970 inflation worked with a ~2-year lag compared to money supply. Money supply slowed dramatically in 2023 and 2024 but has been moving back up. Inflation has also been stickier than the Fed would like, but unfortunately, they cannot do much given the large debt load of the US Government and corporations. Despite inflation staying elevated, even moving up in the recent period, the Fed has no choice but to continue lowering rates.

The inflation rate is now only ~1% below the fed funds rate. Most importantly, the inflation rate is no longer falling like it has been for the last few years. The rate has remained above 2% for 4 years now, proving much stickier than anyone originally forecast. Furthermore, the market is still pricing in a rate cut before a rate hike, all but ensuring the CPI stays elevated.

Historical Perspective

The charts below are designed to put the current trends into historical perspective. The orange bars represent annualized percentage change rather than raw dollar amount. You can see that the money supply is rising at the fastest clip since 2021. This is not a good sign if inflation needs to be coming down.

Below shows the 13-week annualized average over history. This chart overlays the log return of the S&P. Mr. Wenzel proposed that large drops in money supply could be a sign of stock market pullbacks. His theory, derived from Murray Rothbard, states that when the market experiences a shrinking growth rate of money supply (or even negative), it can create liquidity issues in the stock market, leading to a sell-off.

While not a perfect predictive tool, many of the dips in money supply precede market dips. Specifically, the major dips in 2002 and 2008 from +10% down to 0%. 2022 was highly correlated with a fall in money supply, and the rebound has corresponded with the big stock market move we saw in 2023 and into 2024. The slowing growth in 2025 did correlate with the market drop in March/April, but that was clearly more headline-driven.

The market has been very choppy since October. Considering the money supply has continued to grow over that time, the compressed range in the stock markets is likely to result in a move higher once we see a breakout of the current range. Obviously, the war has thrown a monkey wrench into things, but if there is any resolution to the war, it could create a market breakout.

Please note the chart only shows market data through Mar 2nd to align with available M2 data.

One other consideration is the reverse repo market at the Fed. This is a tool that allows financial institutions to swap cash for instruments on the Fed balance sheet.

Reverse repos peaked at $2.55T on Dec 30, 2022. Money gushed out from March 2023 to May 2024. The balance now sits close to zero.

Wrapping Up

The CPI remains above 2%. With a war in the Middle East driving up energy prices and a continued money supply expansion, there is a near certainty that the CPI will be trending up in the months ahead and not down. Then the Fed is in a real pickle. Do they reverse course and start raising rates, leave them unchanged, or continue to cut? What if the labor market weakens? The next Fed chair will be a big driver of policy. Will they prioritize price stability or the labor market? Given the massive debt and over-levered economy, it is almost a certainty that rates will not be raised and the easy money spigots will continue flowing. This could be the final end to the dollar.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Ero Copper: The Correction Finally Came, Time To Step Up And Buy The Dip
data.description:
Introduction

I first heard about ERO Copper (ERO), a small pure-play copper producer with assets in Brazil and headquartered in Canada, in late 2023. Immediately, I became interested in the stock: management had a strong background, the copper bull case was strong, and the company had a compelling growth story.

Since then, the stock price has experienced wild swings as investors have moved from optimistic to pessimistic, over and over again. I find it hard to judge them on the abrupt mood change, as ERO objectively was a leveraged company that was counting on future production to solve its balance sheet problems. The execution risk was indeed too large to ignore.

In the 2023-2024 period, ERO was reporting negative free cash flow as it was heavily investing in its second copper asset, Tucumã. Buying ERO stock at that time meant you had to trust management's ability to successfully deliver a complex mining project on time and with the promised unit economics.

Thankfully for investors, management did exactly as promised. Construction was completed in two years without cost overruns, and Tucumã initiated commercial production in mid-2025. Investors in the small-cap miner space know that when this happens, it is a green flag. It shows management has the skills required to manage capital structure and execution risk in a sector notoriously volatile and unforgiving.

The latest earnings report of 4Q25 provided reassurance of the improving operation. EBITDA rose 147% QoQ and 216% YoY, explained by higher volumes and prices across the board. This drove leverage to 1.2x net debt/EBITDA from 1.9x in 3Q25. The only red flag was cash costs in Tucumã coming in higher than what market participants expected (the problem related to high cash costs will be addressed in more detail later in the article).

Given the recent improvements, the situation in 2026 is completely different from the volatile period of 2023-2024. ERO's two copper assets, Caraíba and Tucumã, are fully operational, and guidance indicates a 2026 copper production of 72,500 tonnes, a 65% increase when compared to 2023. With an average copper price of $9,663/ton in 2025 (lower than the current price of $11,800/t), ERO generated cash flow of $117 million for the year and an adjusted EBITDA of $411 million (graph below).

This means ERO can now weather macro volatility from a position of strength, without the need to raise debt at distressed rates or dilute shareholders with surprise equity issuances at depressed prices.

Company Overview

Let's take a step back and review ERO's back story.

ERO Copper was founded in May 2016 by Christopher Noel Dunn and David Strang (current Executive Chairman). At the time, Dunn had a 25 year background in investment banking, and Strang had a history of successfully buying and selling metal companies in Latin America.

The founders structured the acquisition of Mineração Caraíba, a copper mine that was owned by BNDES (Brazil's development bank) and had a mining history dating back to 1874.

ERO went public on the Toronto Stock Exchange in October 2017, while producing only 20,000 tonnes of copper. Almost 10 years later, the company grew and is on track to become a 100,000-ton copper producer in the future, as suggested by Strang's vision.

Below, I list ERO's assets and growth prospects:

Caraíba Copper Mine

ERO's original asset, acquired in 2016 and located in Bahia state, has consistently produced between 35,000 and 45,000 tons of copper annually since 2019.

The main issue is that with over 40 years of operating history, the mine has been working through progressively lower-grade ore as the shallower, higher-grade zones are depleted—a decline compounded in 2023–2024 by slower-than-expected underground development at the Pilar mine. This drove production from 43,857 tons in 2023 to approximately 36,000 tons in 2025. Management is well aware of that, and the primary capital focus for 2026–2027 is the completion of a large new external shaft at the Pilar mine (image below), which will unlock the high-grade Deepening Extension Zone sitting roughly 1,500 meters below the surface. After the shaft is completed in 2027, Caraíba production is expected to ramp-up to 48,000 tonnes, accompanied by lower cash costs as ore grades improve.

Tucumã

As previously discussed, the Tucumã mine, which is located in Pará state, was the main capital focus in the past couple of years, with commercial production starting in mid 2025. Tucumã is a high grade open pit mine with brand new infrastructure; therefore, it differentiates itself with a low cash cost of $1.95–$2.15 per pound, roughly 15% lower than Caraíba.

2026 is expected to be the first year the mine approaches full nameplate capacity, and company guidance indicates production between 32,500-37,500 tonnes (23% increase YoY at midpoint). Despite ERO's success with the Tucumã project, I can't gloss over the fact that in early 2025, cash cost guidance was $1.05–$1.25, 44% below current values. From what I saw in the annual report, management attributed the increase to lower processed grades as the mine moves through deeper pit material, higher concentrate transportation costs, and BRL currency appreciation. An optimist might argue that cash costs have the potential to go down if the company lowers transportation costs and increases grade quality, but to be conservative, I assume the cash costs will remain elevated at the current levels.

Xavantina

Although ERO is mainly known as a copper producer, when it acquired the owner of the Caraíba mine in 2016, Xavantina came along as part of the package. With depleted reserves and annual production of only ~25,000 ounces, market commentators speculated that management would quickly divest from this non-core asset. What followed was a contrarian bet that paid off handsomely. Exploration drilling identified the Santo Antônio vein—a larger, higher-grade structure than the veins being mined—which restarted the mine's production growth. ERO then launched the NX 60 initiative, centered on developing the newly discovered Matinha vein, with the goal of reaching 60,000 ounces of annual production. Within five years of acquisition, Xavantina had gone from a near-depleted operation with no defined mineral reserves to one producing over 60,000 ounces annually—a reserve compound annual growth rate of ~95% from 2018 to 2022.

Furnas Project

In July 2024, ERO established a partnership with Vale Base Metals on the Furnas Copper-Gold Project in the Carajás Mineral Province in Pará State—one of the world's most prolific mining regions—adding a potentially significant exploration asset to the portfolio. ERO is earning a 60% ownership interest through funding exploration and engineering work programmes. Preliminary Economic Assessment (PEA) results were recently disclosed in Feb-2026. According to the PEA, Furnas is expected to produce more than 1.2 million tons of copper, 2.0 million ounces of gold, and 9 million ounces of silver over its 24 year mine life. To be conservative, I do not account for the Furnas project in my projections. That said, I will follow developments closely, as a successful Furnas development would be transformational, taking ERO well beyond the 100,000-ton annual copper production milestone envisioned by its founders.

Accounting for the developments above, I have arrived at the following production forecast:

Production forecast

With the mentioned completion of the mine shaft in Caraíba and the full ramp-up of Tucumã, I expect copper production to reach 81,000 tonnes in 2027 and 86,000 in 2028. Xavantina gold production should stabilize at 50,000 ounces going forward.

The sections above provided a high-level overview of ERO's assets and growth story. Readers interested in the underlying financial model—including revenue, EBITDA, net income, and capex forecasts through 2028—will find a detailed breakdown in the annex at the end of this article.

The opportunity: ERO is relatively cheap

An attentive reader might ask, is it good to know that in the last couple of years, ERO delivered production growth and an EBITDA CAGR of 21% since 2019?. But why choose ERO to play the copper bull case when there are many other mid-cap miners available?

The answer lies in ERO's quite striking undervaluation compared to its peers. I choose to make a peer analysis using trailing price-to-earnings instead of forward price-to-earnings ratio to avoid having to deal with copper price assumptions analysts are using in the future.

The table shows the three peers chosen (LUNMF, HBM, and TSX:CS) have an average trailing P/E ratio of 23.9x, while ERO has a P/E ratio of only 9.7x. Furthermore, ERO is the company with the highest production growth expected for the near future, so growth does not explain the valuation gap.

What seems to be happening is investors are still viewing ERO as a miner with execution risk, despite the recent developments. All the positive developments described in this article—deleveraging, free cash flow growth, and the successful commissioning of Tucumã—have not yet generated the re-rating that such milestones typically produce.

Therefore, ERO is well positioned to outperform the broader sector by simply maintaining current operating performance.

The path to closing this gap is straightforward: as ERO crosses the sub-1x net debt/EBITDA threshold in 2026 —the milestone that allows the company to initiate a dividend payment program—the market's perception of ERO as a high-risk construction-stage miner should finally give way to its new reality as a cash-generative, growing producer.

The deleveraging bull case is also very consistent with a historical precedent. In the last couple of years, Lundin and Hudbay both strongly outperformed the broader copper mining sector (graph below). Coincidentally, both of them experienced significant deleveraging in recent years and reached a net-debt/EBITDA ratio below 1.0x. Lundin began 2025 with a net debt/EBITDA close to 1.0x and finished at -0.1x, after selling a European asset. Hudbay reduced its net-debt/EBITDA from 1.6x at the end of 2023 to 0.4x today—a journey that coincided with a +288% gain in its share price. ERO, sitting at 1.2x today, is at the beginning of that same journey.

Risks and catalysts to watch

In my opinion, as explained before, the two main catalysts for ERO in the short term are improvements in the net-debt/EBITDA ratio and success in the Caraíba mine shaft, both of them expected to materialize in 2026. A net-debt/EBITDA ratio below 1.0x should solidify ERO as a mature, cash-positive miner, and positive developments in Caraiba's mine shaft will create credibility that the goal of reaching 86,000 copper production in 2028 is attainable.

Positive developments in the Furnas project would also probably generate market interest in the stock; however, management stated recently that a final investment decision will not be made at least until 2028.

The main risk for the stock is quite straightforward. Recently, the market is increasingly worried about a possible worldwide economic recession, driven mainly by consequences of recent geopolitical escalation. Copper prices have already corrected 13% from recent highs, and a further drawdown is possible, if recession worries are not alleviated. In a scenario where copper prices fall below $10,000, analysts will start making downward earnings revisions for the whole sector, and ERO's net debt/EBITDA could stay above 1.0x for longer than I forecasted. To be fair, ERO's stock has already corrected 40% from ATHs, so much of the risk is already embedded in the price.

As previously mentioned, the main red flag I noted while analyzing ERO was the negative surprises regarding cash costs. In my analysis, I forecast copper cash costs at $2.04, in line with management guidance. However, if an inflationary environment drives mining costs higher, there is the risk of cash costs rising relative to revenues, leading to a decrease in gross margins.

Finally, there is the risk of problems arising in the Caraíba mine, given the complexity of the mine shaft construction. There will definitely be a negative stock reaction to unexpected mine disruptions, given the importance of Caraíba's production to ERO's bottom line.

Conclusion

The article showed how ERO developed from a high-execution copper miner, to an established, cash-flow-positive company in the last couple of years.

However, and luckily for investors, the peer analysis section indicates that the expected re-rating is yet to occur. As time goes on and ERO consistently generates cash, net debt/EBITDA will quickly fall below 1.0x, triggering dividends. In my opinion, this is the main catalyst for the stock.

As a bonus, the recent drawdown of over 40% gives investors a significant margin of safety. At current prices, the bull case works even if copper prices fall slightly below 10,000 USD/t. However, if copper prices rally back to ATHs, the potential return is even bigger, considering the impact on earnings (the price target is based on a copper price of 10,000 USD/t).

I initiate coverage of ERO Copper with a BUY recommendation and a price target of $55 in the next twelve months.

I have arrived at the price target by multiplying the forecasted 2027 earnings of $290 million by a price-to-earnings ratio of 20x, in line with sector peers (peer analysis appears in the opportunity section). To arrive at the $290 million figure, I assume a copper price of 10,000 USD/t, copper production of 81,000 tonnes, and overall copper cash cost at 2.04 USD/lb (more details on assumptions can be found in the annex below). With approximately 104 million diluted shares outstanding, this implies earnings per share of approximately $2.79, which at 20x yields a price target of $55.80, rounded to $55. I use 2027 earnings rather than 2026 because 2026 remains a transition year with elevated capex from the Pilar shaft construction. By the time this article is read, the market will increasingly be pricing in 2027 earnings power, making 2027E the more appropriate valuation anchor for a 12-month horizon.

Annex - PnL Forecast

For those who are interested, I show below my financial model for ERO (image below).

Margins remain similar to the 2025 level, as the main driver, which is the copper price, is conservatively forecasted at 10,000 USD/t. The growth in revenue and net income is mainly a result of production reaching 86,000 tons in 2028. It is worth noting that the net-debt/EBITDA level is expected to fall below 1.0x in 2026. This is important because management signaled that reaching this level will allow for dividend distributions.

Some of the main assumptions are the following:

Copper price forecast: Despite copper's run up to above 13,000 USD/t in early 2026, prices fell with current geopolitical tensions and currently sit at between 11,000 and 12,000 USD/t. To be conservative, I assume a copper price of 10,000 USD/t going forward.

Cash cost forecast: Cash costs have risen substantially in the past couple of years. The introduction of the Tucumã project was expected to contain this increase with its high grade ore, however costs are expected to rise even further in 2026. I forecast costs to be maintained at the 2026 level for the next couple of years. However, I understand this is one of the main risks of the thesis. If an inflation spike drives mining costs higher, I will have to adjust my earnings forecast accordingly. Nevertheless, in my defense, sustained inflation should logically also translate to higher copper prices and not only higher costs.

Capex Forecast: ERO's management guided 2026 capex between $275 and $320 million, with approximately $80 million earmarked for the Caraíba shaft project. In 2027, capex is expected to remain moderately elevated as the shaft reaches completion, though the absence of formal guidance beyond 2026 means my estimate of approximately $250–280 million for that year is my own. After 2027, with no major greenfield projects assumed in the model, capex should normalize toward approximately $150 million annually as ERO transitions from a growth to a maintenance capital regime—a meaningful reduction that should translate directly into higher free cash flow generation.

data.title: Broadcom: The AI Thesis Is Playing Out Faster Than Expected
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In my last article on Broadcom Inc. (NASDAQ: (AVGO)), I initiated coverage on the name by analyzing its Q1 report and by evaluating whether investors were right to be cautious on the name. I had a BUY rating on the name.

Since the article was published in December 2025, the stock has lost 8.73%, underperforming the S&P 500, which lost 5% during the same period.

The question then becomes: has anything substantially changed when it comes to the AI revolution in general and AVGO’s business in particular, which has led to such a drastic negative sentiment? Have the long-term growth prospects of AVGO dimmed either due to deteriorating demand for its chips or the Tomahawk 6?

In this article, I evaluate the company’s Q1 report and dissect the aforementioned questions and re-evaluate the long-term prospects of AVGO. I also deploy an upgraded ensemble valuation model to compute the company’s updated price target.

AVGO’s AI Business Enters the Acceleration Phase

In my previous article, I argued how 2025 was the year when AVGO’s business had reached an inflection point. If the latest quarter was anything to go by, then 2026, in my view, should be the year when the business transitions from an inflection point to an acceleration phase. For instance, in Q1, the company’s AI semiconductor revenues saw a 106% jump y/y, coming in at $8.4 billion, surpassing management’s own guidance of $8.2 billion. The guidance was even more stunning. More specifically, management now expects Q2 AI semiconductor revenues of $10.7 billion, which would represent a y/y growth of 140%, a remarkable sequential growth in my opinion.

Moreover, CEO Hock Tan also announced that the company sees AI chip revenues of over $100 billion in 2027, which to me, is one of the most significant takeaways from AVGO’s latest quarter. Critically, management announced that the company has secured the supply chain capacity through 2028, which lends further support to both the company's near-term and long-term growth projections.

Management’s long-term visibility can also be supported by two concurrent forces: the strong training cycle, which continues to remain strong as hyperscalers continue to compete against each other to develop state-of-the-art LLM models; and the demand for inference, which is showing tremendous growth as the hyperscalers simultaneously focus on agentic AI. Management confirmed the presence of these concurrent forces during the earnings call, announcing that they are seeing their customers investing simultaneously in chips required for both training and inference.

Overall, when one takes the blowout Q1 report and the Q2 guidance, along with the clear long-term visibility, it becomes clear that AVGO is fast moving from being one of the beneficiaries of the AI revolution to one of its primary architects, alongside (NVDA).

Q1 Confirms Tomahawk 6’s Game-Changing Ability & Tomahawk 7 is on the Horizon

In my previous article, I had described the Tomahawk 6 switch as a potential gamechanger for AVGO’s networking business. This thesis was confirmed in the Q1 report, as evidenced by the 60% y/y jump in the company’s AI networking revenue, representing about a third of the overall AI revenue. Management also announced that by Q2, the AI networking revenue should account for 40% of the overall revenue, further lending support to the accelerated growth brought about by Tomahawk 6. Notably, should the company hit the 40% figure, it would translate to revenues of about $40 billion, which would be higher than the overall revenue of peers such as CSCO or Arista.

The Tomahawk 6 is the world’s first and only switch that is capable of a bandwidth in excess of 100 Tb per second. When combined with AVGO’s 200G SerDes, the only offering at that speed that is currently in production, one can see that AVGO has a monopoly when it comes to AI networking silicon.

Crucially, from a long-term perspective, management also announced that the next iteration, Tomahawk 7 is set to be launched in 2027, and is expected to have double the performance of TH6. Management also highlighted that the company’s unique ability to enable direct attached copper [DAC] connectivity through both 200G and 400G SerDes gives it a significant advantage over optical alternatives with respect to latency, power consumption, and cost.

Finally, from a structural standpoint, the bull case for AVGO’s networking business has also evolved. More specifically, management announced that Ethernet has is, in all likelihood, set to become the de facto standard for both scale-out and scale-up AI networking, which once again offers a significant tailwind to AVGO’s networking business.

Valuation

Forward P/E Approach

Price Target

Projected Forward P/E Multiple

Projected FY26 EPS

$9.82

Source: Author’s Gradient-Boosted Stacking Ensemble Model

My price target for AVGO was calculated using the forward P/E multiple approach. To calculate the projected EPS, I deployed a gradient-boosted stacking ensemble model, which represents a significant upgrade over the hybrid ensemble model that I used in my last article.

The gradient-boosted stacking ensemble model retained the regime-switching dynamic growth model [now referred to as the Momentum-decay model], and the random forest machine learning model. However, the single random forest model is replaced by three gradient-boosted machine learning models. These include the XGBoost, the LightGBM, and the random forest models. The models are combined using a dynamic weighting layer, which ensures that higher weights are automatically allocated to those models that have a lower forecast error.

More specifically, the components of the upgraded ensemble model are as follows:

  • Momentum-Decay Model – As I mentioned in my last article, AVGO’s business has undergone a significant transition since the onset of the AI revolution, with the company’s networking & semiconductor segments now being the major drivers of the top- and bottom-lines. The momentum-decay model detects the structural break in AVGO’s earnings trend. It then forecasts how the current momentum decays towards a data-driven sustainable growth rate, which has been derived from the company’s own 3-year median growth rate (23% annual). Put simply, the model serves as the “gravity anchor” in the ensemble and ensures that the overall forecast does not extrapolate unsustainably.
  • XGBoost (Gradient Boosted Trees) – this model is a supervised machine learning algorithm. The model builds decision trees sequentially, with each new tree specifically trained to correct the errors of the previous ensemble. This model is more effective for noisy financial data. Moreover, the model ranked AVGO’s operating margin trend as its most important feature, consistent with the thesis that the primary earnings driver is the operating leverage from the AI semiconductor ramp.
  • LightGBM (Leaf-Wise Gradient Boosting) – this machine learning model is similar to the XGBoost. The key difference is that unlike the XGBoost, this model uses leaf-wise tree growth (splitting the most informative leaf at each step), which produces different decision boundaries compared to XGBoost, thereby providing the ensemble with model diversity, subsequently reducing the overall prediction error. The model ranked AVGO’s earnings stability (4-quarter rolling volatility) as its top feature, which essentially means that it has the tendency to produce more conservative forecasts.
  • Recursive Random Forest Machine Learning Model – this model is a supervised machine learning algorithm. The model was trained on AVGO and its peers with the peer group consisting of a mix of semis (NVDA, (AMD), (MRVL), (TXN), (ADI)) as well as a group of infrastructure software players ((CSCO), (ORCL), (IBM), (ANET)). This mix of peers allows the machine learning model to understand AVGO’s growth trajectory from the perspective of the company being a chip supplier as well as being a software platform. The “recursive” component of the model ensures that it is able to dynamically adjust to the sector-wide shifts, such as the AI capex cycle.

All three machine learning models were trained on a feature set that included 12 predictive signals across three categories:

  1. Momentum – EPS Growth (q/q and y/y), Growth Acceleration, Earnings Volatility
  2. Fundamentals – Revenue Growth, Operating Margin Trend, Free Cash Flow Intensity, Leverage Trend
  3. Macroeconomic signals – GDP Growth, Fed Funds rate, 10-year T-yield, and VIX

The expanded feature set allows the machine learning models to distinguish between differing scenarios (e.g., EPS growth driven by margin expansion vs. EPS growth driven by a revenue surge with compressing margins)

I undertook a walk-forward validation test for all four models to determine their accuracy. More specifically, the forecasting accuracy was determined over 12 distinct windows (last three years). On each occasion, the model was trained only on the data prior to a specific quarter and was subsequently asked to predict the following four quarters. Critically, data leakage was eliminated by re-fitting the data normalization inside each fold using only historically available data, which ensures that the reported error metrics are genuinely out-of-sample.

The model evaluation metrics are as follows:

To further improve the predecessor model, I deployed an inverse-MAPE weighting scheme instead of manually assigning fixed weights. The approach automatically allocates a higher ensemble weight to those models with a lower forecast error.

The resulting allocation was:

  1. LightGBM – 30.5% (a higher weight implies that the model naturally gravitates towards conservative, high-confidence predictions, which is a positive signal)
  2. XGBoost – 26.0%

The ensemble model’s forward 4Q EPS of $9.82, implies a growth in excess of 30%. In my view, this is reasonable given the increased visibility of the AI chip revenue in 2027, as well as the continued surge in demand for TH6.

Moreover, the updated EPS is higher than the previous forecast of $9.01 due to two reasons: (1) AVGO’s Q1 FY26 actual EPS of $2.05 beat estimates, which confirmed the acceleration trajectory, and (2) the upgraded model’s ability to capture the margin expansion signals through the fundamental features

It is important to note here that the forecasts from individual models exhibit meaningful divergence, which is considered to be a healthy sign for an ensemble. The ensemble model blends the perspectives from the most aggressive model [XGBoost] and the most conservative model [LightGBM], which subsequently results in an overall forecast that is more robust than any individual model.

I also deployed conformal prediction intervals to quantify the uncertainty around the point estimate of the ensemble model. These also help to determine the projected EPS in the bear and bull case scenarios. The intervals were derived from 48 out-of-sample residuals across the 12-fold walk-forward validation, and are as follows:

The price target under the Base case scenario implies an upside of about 45% from current levels. The price target under the Bull Case scenario implies an upside of about 66% from current levels while the Bear Case scenario suggests a downside of about 20% from current levels.

The risk/reward ratio, which is computed as the Base Case upside divided by the Bear Case downside, comes out to be 2.25x, which comfortably exceeds the 2:1 threshold for a high conviction buy. As such, I am upgrading my rating on AVGO from a BUY to a STRONG BUY.

Having said that, given that the stock’s near-term momentum has marginally weakened, I continue to recommend a dollar cost averaging [DCA] for the name.

Risk Factors

The primary risk factor would be customer concentration. AVGO’s AI business is driven by just 6 customers, which also includes (OPENAI) and Anthropic. While the hyperscalers such as (GOOGL) and (META) may have deep pockets, the same cannot be said about the parents of ChatGPT and Claude respectively. As such, any potential deterioration in orders would be a significant threat to my bull case.

The other risk to my bull case would be the Regulatory Overhang over VMware. The antitrust complaint from the Cloud Infrastructure Service Providers in Europe [CISPE], which accused AVGO of market dominance following it’s acquisition of VMware, as well as the announcement from China’s Cyberspace Administration [CAC], which instructs state-owned enterprises to phase out foreign software, including VMware, also pose potential headwinds to my bull case.

Concluding Thoughts

AVGO had yet another strong quarter, with a beat on both the top- and bottom-line estimates. The company’s AI business has firmly entered the acceleration phase. The increased visibility of AI revenues as well the company’s moves to lock up the supply chain required to meet the visibility should offer investors encouragement regarding the long-term growth of the company’s business. Furthermore, the demand for Tomahawk 6 continues to surge, and with TH7 on the horizon, AVGO’s near-monopoly in the segment is unlikely to be threatened anytime soon.

From a valuation perspective, I have upgraded my rating from a BUY to a STRONG BUY given that the risk-reward ratio at current levels comfortably exceeds the 2:1 threshold. However, given that the sentiment towards the AI buildout continues to be on a shaky ground and given that the stock's momentum has weakened, a DCA would still be the ideal strategy in my opinion when it comes to building a position in the name.

I continue to believe that 2026 would be a breakout year for AVGO. On the basis of the company's Q1 report and guidance, 2027 could be a blockbuster one too.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Henkel Adds Shine With Olaplex
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Olaplex (OLPX) to be acquired by Henkel (HENKY) for $1.4B; stock surges 49% premarket. (00:14) JetBlue (JBLU) jumps on report it's exploring potential sale. (01:14) LA jury finds Meta, Google liable for inducing social media addiction in young woman. (02:05)

This is an abridged transcript.

Olaplex Holdings (OLPX) is up 49% in premarket action.

Olaplex agreed to be acquired by German consumer giant Henkel AG & Co. KGaA (HENKY) (HELKF) (HENOY).

The all-cash transaction is valued at $1.4B.

The $2.06 per share cash offer represents a massive 55% premium over Olaplex’s March 25 closing price, and a premium of approximately 45% over the volume weighted average price for the last 30 trading days.

Upon completion of the deal, expected in the second half of 2026, Olaplex will be delisted from the Nasdaq but will continue to operate as a standalone brand within Henkel’s professional hair care portfolio.

Notably, Advent International, which holds a majority of the voting power in Olaplex, has already provided written consent for the transaction, so no further action is needed from other stockholders. Advent will fully exit its investment upon closing.

JetBlue (JBLU) closed the day Wednesday up 13% on a report that it's evaluating plans to sell itself to a rival airline.

According to a Semafor report on Wednesday, which cited people familiar with the matter, JetBlue (JBLU) has tapped advisers to see how a deal with United Airlines (UAL), Alaska Airlines (ALK) or Southwest Airlines (LUV) might be perceived in Washington.

According to the report, this is preliminary, and the airline may decide to keep the status quo and not try to sell itself. It wasn't known whether JetBlue (JBLU) had held any talks or received indications of interest.

JetBlue declined to comment on internal talks to Semafor.

United (UAL) and Southwest (LUV) also declined to comment. Alaska Air (ALK) didn’t immediately respond to a request for comment.

Shares of rival airlines rose after the Semafor report.

A jury in Los Angeles determined Wednesday that Meta Platforms (META) and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) were responsible for inducing addictive behavior in a young woman.

She began using social media platforms as a child. The now 20-year-old woman alleged that she became addicted to apps like Instagram and YouTube due to the appearance of the platforms.

She said household social media platforms caused her to develop anxiety, body dysmorphia, and suicidal thoughts. TikTok (BDNCE) and Snap (SNAP) were also part of the suit, but they settled before the case went to court.

The jury ordered compensatory damages of $3M to be awarded to the plaintiff, of which Meta is expected to pay 70% and Google the remaining. Additional punitive damages are also being considered.

A Meta spokesperson said, "We respectfully disagree with the verdict and are evaluating our legal options."

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

Trump team reportedly exploring implications of oil reaching $200 per barrel

Iran said to draft bill to impose fees for transit through Strait of Hormuz

Super Micro faces lawsuit alleging securities fraud tied to China sales

Catalyst watch:

  • Shareholders with Heritage Commerce (HTBK) and CVB Financial (CVBF) vote on their all-stock deal valued at about $811M.
